content.text: What a Change Impact Assessment Is

A change impact assessment (CIA) maps how a GenAI adoption changes the day-to-day work of each affected role and process area. It answers three questions for each combination: What specifically changes? How significant is the change? What does the person need (training, process redesign, accountability clarification) to perform in the new state? Without this mapping, OCM planning defaults to generic communications and training that do not address the actual disruption experienced by each group.

A CIA is not a list of features. It is a role-by-role account of what changes in the lived experience of doing the job — which tasks disappear, which new tasks appear, which skills become critical, and which accountabilities shift.
Change Impact Heat Map

attrs.alt: Change Impact Heat Map — GenAI Adoption by Role × Process Area

content.text: The heat map above shows impact magnitude across five roles and six process areas for a typical mid-scale GenAI programme. HIGH cells require significant workflow redesign, new training, and accountability clarification. LOW cells may need only a brief awareness communication. Reading across rows reveals which roles are most disrupted; reading down columns reveals which process areas drive the most change.
Conducting the Assessment
A CIA for GenAI adoption has four inputs: process inventories, role descriptions, the GenAI tool's capability scope, and interviews or workshops with role representatives.
Step 1: Map Current-State Tasks
For each role in scope, enumerate the tasks performed in the affected process areas. Classify each task by type:

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional


class TaskType(Enum):
    COGNITIVE = "cognitive"          # analysis, judgement, writing
    PROCEDURAL = "procedural"        # follow a defined process, form filling
    RELATIONAL = "relational"        # communication, stakeholder management
    OVERSIGHT = "oversight"          # review and sign-off


class AIImpact(Enum):
    AUTOMATES = "automates"          # AI performs this task with human oversight
    AUGMENTS = "augments"            # AI assists; human still performs core task
    UNCHANGED = "unchanged"          # AI does not materially affect this task
    CREATES_NEW = "creates_new"      # new task created by AI adoption


@dataclass
class RoleTask:
    role: str
    process_area: str
    task_name: str
    task_type: TaskType
    time_pct: float              # % of working week
    ai_impact: AIImpact
    new_skill_required: Optional[str] = None
    accountability_change: str = ""  # "" if unchanged

    def impact_score(self) -> float:
        """Numeric impact: higher = more significant change."""
        ai_weights = {
            AIImpact.AUTOMATES: 0.9,
            AIImpact.AUGMENTS: 0.5,
            AIImpact.CREATES_NEW: 0.7,
            AIImpact.UNCHANGED: 0.0,
        }
        return self.time_pct * ai_weights[self.ai_impact]


# Legal Analyst task inventory (partial)
tasks = [
    RoleTask(
        role="Legal Analyst",
        process_area="Content Creation",
        task_name="Draft contract summary sections",
        task_type=TaskType.COGNITIVE,
        time_pct=25.0,
        ai_impact=AIImpact.AUGMENTS,
        new_skill_required="AI output validation; prompt engineering for legal domain",
        accountability_change="Analyst remains accountable; must document validation steps",
    ),
    RoleTask(
        role="Legal Analyst",
        process_area="Content Creation",
        task_name="Extract key clauses from NDAs",
        task_type=TaskType.PROCEDURAL,
        time_pct=15.0,
        ai_impact=AIImpact.AUTOMATES,
        new_skill_required="Hallucination detection for clause extraction",
        accountability_change="AI produces extraction; analyst validates and signs off",
    ),
    RoleTask(
        role="Legal Analyst",
        process_area="Decision Support",
        task_name="Flag risk clauses for partner review",
        task_type=TaskType.COGNITIVE,
        time_pct=10.0,
        ai_impact=AIImpact.AUGMENTS,
        new_skill_required="Calibrate trust in AI-flagged vs. missed risks",
        accountability_change="Analyst retains full accountability; AI provides first-pass only",
    ),
    RoleTask(
        role="Legal Analyst",
        process_area="Compliance Review",
        task_name="Check contract against regulatory checklist",
        task_type=TaskType.PROCEDURAL,
        time_pct=12.0,
        ai_impact=AIImpact.AUTOMATES,
        new_skill_required="Validate AI compliance check output against authoritative source",
        accountability_change="Analyst must still sign compliance certificate",
    ),
    RoleTask(
        role="Legal Analyst",
        process_area="Customer Comms",
        task_name="Client email drafting",
        task_type=TaskType.RELATIONAL,
        time_pct=8.0,
        ai_impact=AIImpact.AUGMENTS,
        new_skill_required=None,
        accountability_change="",
    ),
]

# Impact ranking
ranked = sorted(tasks, key=lambda t: t.impact_score(), reverse=True)
print("Legal Analyst — Tasks ranked by impact score:")
for t in ranked:
    changed = "↑ accountability change" if t.accountability_change else ""
    skill = f" | new skill: {t.new_skill_required[:40]}..." if t.new_skill_required else ""
    print(f"  [{t.impact_score():.1f}] {t.task_name} ({t.ai_impact.value}){skill}{' | ' + changed if changed else ''}")
Step 2: Score Impact Magnitude

Impact magnitude for a task combines three factors: the time it currently occupies (higher time = higher impact when it changes), the degree of AI involvement (automation > augmentation), and whether it carries an accountability shift (resets the risk profile of the role).

from dataclasses import dataclass


@dataclass
class ImpactScore:
    role: str
    process_area: str
    workflow_change_score: float    # 0–10: how much the daily workflow changes
    accountability_shift_score: float  # 0–10: how much accountability responsibility changes
    skill_gap_score: float          # 0–10: magnitude of new skills required
    weight_workflow: float = 0.4
    weight_accountability: float = 0.35
    weight_skill: float = 0.25

    def composite(self) -> float:
        return (
            self.workflow_change_score * self.weight_workflow
            + self.accountability_shift_score * self.weight_accountability
            + self.skill_gap_score * self.weight_skill
        )

    def magnitude_label(self) -> str:
        c = self.composite()
        if c >= 6.5:
            return "HIGH"
        if c >= 3.5:
            return "MEDIUM"
        return "LOW"


scores = [
    ImpactScore("Legal Analyst", "Content Creation",   8.0, 7.5, 8.0),
    ImpactScore("Legal Analyst", "Compliance Review",  7.0, 9.0, 7.5),
    ImpactScore("Legal Analyst", "Decision Support",   6.5, 8.0, 6.0),
    ImpactScore("SW Engineer",   "Code / Dev",         8.5, 6.0, 7.0),
    ImpactScore("Customer Support", "Customer Comms",  7.5, 5.5, 4.0),
    ImpactScore("Manager",       "Decision Support",   5.0, 8.5, 4.5),
]

print("Impact scorecard:")
for s in sorted(scores, key=lambda x: x.composite(), reverse=True):
    print(f"  {s.magnitude_label()} [{s.composite():.1f}] {s.role} × {s.process_area}")
Step 3: Identify Required Interventions
For each HIGH and MEDIUM cell, document the specific interventions required. Avoid generic entries like "training needed" — specificity makes the intervention actionable.
Role × Process Area
Impact
Workflow Intervention
Training Intervention
Accountability Intervention
Legal Analyst × Compliance Review
HIGH
Insert mandatory AI validation checkpoint before compliance certificate sign-off
4-hour workshop: AI compliance check output anatomy; known error modes; authoritative source cross-check method
Written policy: analyst remains signatory; AI output is advisory only
Legal Analyst × Content Creation
HIGH
New task: prompt drafting and output review added to contract workflow
AI prompt engineering for legal domain; hallucination spotting in clause extraction
Accountability unchanged; validation log required for audit trail
SW Engineer × Code / Dev
HIGH
Code review checklist updated to include AI-generated code patterns; pair-review for AI-assisted PRs
AI code security patterns; common LLM code anti-patterns; when to override AI suggestion
Engineer retains full accountability for merged code regardless of AI origin
Manager × Decision Support
HIGH
Manager sign-off on AI-assisted reports requires evidence of validation by report author
AI report interpretation: confidence indicators, source tracing, what to question
Manager accountability for decisions extends to AI-generated inputs used in those decisions
Process-Level Change Impact

In addition to role-level impacts, assess the process itself. Some processes require structural redesign when GenAI is introduced — inserting a new validation step, adding an audit trail, or splitting a task that was previously done by one person into an AI-performs + human-validates pair.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum


class ProcessChangeType(Enum):
    STEP_ADDED = "step_added"
    STEP_REMOVED = "step_removed"
    STEP_REDESIGNED = "step_redesigned"
    ACCOUNTABILITY_SPLIT = "accountability_split"   # AI does; human validates
    AUDIT_TRAIL_REQUIRED = "audit_trail_required"


@dataclass
class ProcessChange:
    process_name: str
    change_type: ProcessChangeType
    description: str
    downstream_effects: list[str]
    requires_policy_update: bool

    def summary(self) -> str:
        effects = "; ".join(self.downstream_effects) if self.downstream_effects else "none"
        policy = " [POLICY UPDATE NEEDED]" if self.requires_policy_update else ""
        return (
            f"[{self.change_type.value}] {self.process_name}: {self.description}"
            f"\n  Downstream: {effects}{policy}"
        )


process_changes = [
    ProcessChange(
        process_name="NDA Review Process",
        change_type=ProcessChangeType.ACCOUNTABILITY_SPLIT,
        description="AI performs clause extraction (steps 2–4); analyst validates extraction and signs off (new step 4a)",
        downstream_effects=[
            "Step 4a validation must be logged in contract management system",
            "SLA for NDA turnaround updated (faster extraction, same validation time)",
        ],
        requires_policy_update=True,
    ),
    ProcessChange(
        process_name="Code Review Process",
        change_type=ProcessChangeType.STEP_ADDED,
        description="AI-generated code sections flagged in PR; reviewer applies AI code checklist before approval",
        downstream_effects=["PR template updated to include AI-origin flag", "Review SLA unchanged"],
        requires_policy_update=False,
    ),
    ProcessChange(
        process_name="Management Reporting",
        change_type=ProcessChangeType.AUDIT_TRAIL_REQUIRED,
        description="AI-drafted report sections must include source reference and validation attestation by author",
        downstream_effects=["Report template updated", "Author sign-off section added"],
        requires_policy_update=True,
    ),
]

for pc in process_changes:
    print(pc.summary())
Common CIA Mistakes
Assessing impact at department level rather than role level
— saying "Legal will be impacted" gives no actionable information. Different roles within Legal (analyst, paralegal, partner) have entirely different impacts.
Confusing tool features with workflow changes

— listing "AI can extract clauses" is a feature, not an impact. The impact is "analysts spend 15% less time on extraction and must spend that time on validation instead" — that is what changes in their working day.
Missing accountability shifts

— the most consequential changes for professional roles are often not workflow steps but accountability boundaries. An analyst who signs off AI-generated compliance output without the assessment noting this explicitly will be unprepared for the accountability shift.
No process-level assessment

— role-level impacts sum to process-level change, but processes sometimes require redesign that is not visible from any single role's perspective. The NDA review process must be assessed as a whole, not just as a Legal Analyst task list.
Validating the Assessment with Role Representatives

A CIA produced entirely by programme staff without role representation is unreliable. The people in each role know their actual task distribution, informal workarounds, and professional accountability concerns better than any external observer. Two validation steps are essential:
Workshop Validation
Run a 90-minute workshop with 4–6 representatives from each HIGH-impact role group. Present the draft heat map and task-level impact assessment. Ask:
Which tasks did we miss or misrepresent?
Where have we underestimated the impact? Where have we overestimated it?
What accountability concern is most important to your professional practice that we have not captured?
Is there a task you currently do informally (not in any process map) that AI will significantly change?

Informal tasks are routinely missed by CIA exercises because they do not appear in process documentation. A legal analyst who informally mentors junior staff by checking their clause extractions will have a different AI impact experience than the process map suggests.
Spot-Check Calibration

After workshops, calibrate impact scores against objective data where available. If the programme has a pilot group, compare the CIA's predicted impact magnitude against the actual behaviour change observed in the pilot (ADKAR stage distribution, time-on-task measurements, error rates).

from dataclasses import dataclass


@dataclass
class ImpactCalibration:
    role: str
    process_area: str
    predicted_magnitude: str     # "HIGH", "MEDIUM", "LOW"
    pilot_adkar_stage_4wk: str   # ADKAR stage observed at 4 weeks for pilot group
    pilot_error_rate_change: float  # % change in error rate vs pre-AI baseline
    calibrated_magnitude: str    # updated after calibration

    def calibration_note(self) -> str:
        if self.predicted_magnitude != self.calibrated_magnitude:
            return (
                f"RECALIBRATED {self.predicted_magnitude} → {self.calibrated_magnitude}: "
                f"pilot ADKAR at {self.pilot_adkar_stage_4wk}, "
                f"error rate {self.pilot_error_rate_change:+.0f}%"
            )
        return f"CONFIRMED {self.calibrated_magnitude}"


calibrations = [
    ImpactCalibration(
        role="Legal Analyst",
        process_area="Content Creation",
        predicted_magnitude="HIGH",
        pilot_adkar_stage_4wk="ABILITY",
        pilot_error_rate_change=-12.0,
        calibrated_magnitude="HIGH",
    ),
    ImpactCalibration(
        role="Legal Analyst",
        process_area="Compliance Review",
        predicted_magnitude="HIGH",
        pilot_adkar_stage_4wk="KNOWLEDGE",   # lower than expected
        pilot_error_rate_change=+5.0,         # errors increased
        calibrated_magnitude="HIGH",
    ),
    ImpactCalibration(
        role="Customer Support",
        process_area="Customer Comms",
        predicted_magnitude="HIGH",
        pilot_adkar_stage_4wk="ABILITY",
        pilot_error_rate_change=-8.0,
        calibrated_magnitude="MEDIUM",        # pilot adoption faster than expected
    ),
]

print("Impact calibration against pilot data:")
for c in calibrations:
    print(f"  {c.role} × {c.process_area}: {c.calibration_note()}")
CIA Output: The Intervention Registry

The CIA produces an intervention registry — a structured list of specific, assigned actions that the OCM team tracks through to completion. Each intervention is linked to the role × process combination that generated it and to the ADKAR stage it targets.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional


class ADKARTarget(Enum):
    AWARENESS = "awareness"
    DESIRE = "desire"
    KNOWLEDGE = "knowledge"
    ABILITY = "ability"
    REINFORCEMENT = "reinforcement"


class InterventionType(Enum):
    COMMUNICATION = "communication"
    TRAINING = "training"
    WORKFLOW_REDESIGN = "workflow_redesign"
    POLICY_UPDATE = "policy_update"
    ACCOUNTABILITY_CLARIFICATION = "accountability_clarification"
    COACHING = "coaching"


@dataclass
class Intervention:
    role: str
    process_area: str
    impact_magnitude: str
    adkar_target: ADKARTarget
    type_: InterventionType
    description: str
    owner: str
    due_date: str
    complete: bool = False

    def priority(self) -> int:
        """Sort order: HIGH impact + early ADKAR stages first."""
        mag = {"HIGH": 0, "MEDIUM": 1, "LOW": 2}.get(self.impact_magnitude, 2)
        stage = list(ADKARTarget).index(self.adkar_target)
        return mag * 10 + stage


@dataclass
class InterventionRegistry:
    programme_id: str
    interventions: list[Intervention] = field(default_factory=list)

    def add(self, i: Intervention) -> None:
        self.interventions.append(i)

    def pending(self) -> list[Intervention]:
        return [i for i in self.interventions if not i.complete]

    def by_owner(self) -> dict[str, list[Intervention]]:
        result: dict[str, list[Intervention]] = {}
        for i in self.interventions:
            result.setdefault(i.owner, []).append(i)
        return result

    def summary(self) -> str:
        pending = self.pending()
        return (
            f"Intervention Registry: {len(pending)}/{len(self.interventions)} pending | "
            f"{len(self.by_owner())} owners"
        )


registry = InterventionRegistry(programme_id="legal-summariser-rollout")
registry.add(Intervention(
    role="Legal Analyst",
    process_area="Compliance Review",
    impact_magnitude="HIGH",
    adkar_target=ADKARTarget.KNOWLEDGE,
    type_=InterventionType.TRAINING,
    description="4h workshop: AI compliance output anatomy, known error modes, cross-check with authoritative source",
    owner="L&D Lead",
    due_date="2026-07-10",
))
registry.add(Intervention(
    role="Legal Analyst",
    process_area="Compliance Review",
    impact_magnitude="HIGH",
    adkar_target=ADKARTarget.AWARENESS,
    type_=InterventionType.ACCOUNTABILITY_CLARIFICATION,
    description="Written policy: analyst remains signatory on compliance certificates; AI output is advisory",
    owner="General Counsel",
    due_date="2026-07-01",
))
registry.add(Intervention(
    role="Manager",
    process_area="Decision Support",
    impact_magnitude="HIGH",
    adkar_target=ADKARTarget.ABILITY,
    type_=InterventionType.COACHING,
    description="Manager 1:1 coaching on how to run a validation conversation with report authors using AI",
    owner="OCM Lead",
    due_date="2026-07-15",
))

print(registry.summary())
for i in sorted(registry.pending(), key=lambda x: x.priority()):
    print(f"  [{i.impact_magnitude}] {i.role} × {i.process_area} [{i.adkar_target.value}]")
    print(f"    {i.type_.value}: {i.description[:60]}...")
    print(f"    Owner: {i.owner} | Due: {i.due_date}")
Keeping the CIA Current
A CIA produced at programme start becomes stale as GenAI capabilities change and as the organisation learns from pilot experience. Update the CIA at two points:
After the pilot
— compare predicted impact scores against observed pilot data. Recalibrate HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW assignments before full rollout. Adjust interventions where the pilot revealed gaps.
On model change

— when the underlying model is upgraded or replaced, reassess any task classified as AUTOMATES. A model change may shift a task from AUTOMATES back to AUGMENTS (reduced capability) or introduce new automation coverage that changes the workflow impact.
The CIA is a living document, not a one-time artefact. Assign a named owner (typically the OCM lead) who is responsible for scheduling and executing updates at each review point.
Summary

A change impact assessment for GenAI adoption maps current-state tasks per role, scores each on three dimensions (workflow change, accountability shift, skill gap), produces a heat map that prioritises OCM investment, and documents the specific training, workflow, and policy interventions required. HIGH-impact cells at the role × process intersection drive the OCM plan's design; LOW-impact cells may need only an awareness communication. The discipline that distinguishes an effective CIA from a checklist exercise is specificity: each intervention is described in concrete, actionable terms rather than generic categories.
